steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Form, Structure, and Sense question testing subject-verb agreement. The subject is "Latvia's 4,917-word constitution" — a singular noun phrase. The long intervening clause "in contrast to Chile's, which was enacted in 1980 and contains a far greater number (25,821) of words" is a parenthetical and does not change the subject's number. The verb must agree with a singular subject.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'ranks': Third-person singular present tense. Agrees with the singular subject "Latvia's 4,917-word constitution." Grammatically correct. ✓
  • Choice B — 'have ranked': Present perfect plural form. "Have" requires a plural subject; "constitution" is singular. Subject-verb disagreement. ✗
  • Choice C — 'rank': Bare plural verb form. This would agree with a plural subject, but "constitution" is singular. Subject-verb disagreement. ✗
  • Choice D — 'are ranking': Present progressive plural. "Are" requires a plural subject; "constitution" is singular. Subject-verb disagreement. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
